article.title: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) in Tobacco: Isolation of Activated Genes Suggests Role of the Phenylpropanoid Pathway in Inhibition of Bacterial Pathogens
article.license: This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License, which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are properly credited.

events.event_label: cinnamic acid and p-coumaric acid detected in tobacco leaf tissue
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p085.s001: A) Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC) separation and densitometric measurement of two phenolic compounds, cinnamic acid and p-coumaric acid in PTI-induced and control tobacco leaf tissue. PMC4125134.p085.s002: Tobacco leaves were treated with dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), piperonylic acid (PIP), P. syringae hrcC- bacteria (hrcC), their combination (PIP+hrcC), flagellin 22 peptide (flg22) and combination of piperonylic acid and flagellin 22 peptide (PIP+flg22). PMC4125134.p085.s003: Tissue samples (500 mg) were collected 6 hours after infiltration. PMC4125134.p085.s004: Separation and detection of p-coumaric acid and cinnamic acid was carried out by thin layer chromatography (TLC) and UV densitometry.

events.event_label: PIP inhibits C4H leading to increased cinnamate
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p086.s006: Looking at pairs of treatment X and treatment X+PIP, the largest difference was found between hrcC and hrcC+PIP treatment among our experimental settings, and difference between flg22 and flg22+PIP was also significant. PMC4125134.p086.s007: Knowing that PAL, the previous enzyme in the phenylpropanoid pathway is also activated upon PTI elicitor treatment, one might hypothesize that when elicitors are added, PAL creates elevated levels of cinnamate, which is then normally turned into coumarate by C4H. PMC4125134.p086.s008: This transformation is then stuck by the inhibitor PIP, resulting in high levels of cinnamate.

events.event_label: 6 hpi upregulates POX and F5H expression
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p101.s006: Among our experimental settings, each gene was activated (Figure 3C), however to a lesser extent than in response to the P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p101.s007: syringae hrcC- bacteria (Figure 3B). PMC4125134.p101.s008: The response to flg22 seemed to fade away earlier as well. PMC4125134.p101.s009: At 3 hpi each investigated phenylpropanoid gene was activated significantly, while at 6 hpi only two of them: POX (C72) and F5H (C39). PMC4125134.p101.s010: One probable reason for this difference might be that whole bacterial cells contain many other PTI elicitors resulting in a stronger PTI reaction than flagellin alone.

events.event_label: hrcC mutant abolishes HR and enhances PTI-related response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p017.s001: Hrp/hrc mutants of the Pseudomonas syringae pv. syringae 61 bacterial strain (original wild type causing brown spot on bean and HR on tobacco) are damaged in their ability to cause disease on bean or HR on tobacco to varying extent, depending on which hrp gene is turned off. PMC4125134.p017.s002: The hrcC mutant is especially useful for investigating PTI, as its ability to induce HR in tobacco is completely abolished, while the induced PTI can nicely be detected by an HR-inhibition test, and it is also a potent elicitor of PTI-related genes.

events.event_label: flg22 peptide induces PTI in Arabidopsis leaves
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p094.s001: Several authors ( ) studied the transcriptomic changes during PTI, mostly in Arabidopsis model plants. PMC4125134.p094.s002: Although exact parameters (bacterium strains, times of sampling) are different, the numbers of activated genes are comparable. PMC4125134.p094.s003: Treatments inducing PTI in planta, such as injection of flg22 peptide or infection with P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p094.s004: tomato DC3000 hrpA resulted in activation of 220–250 genes within 0.5–7 hours according to the above authors.

events.event_label: PTI alters cinnamic acid and p-coumaric acid levels
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p084.s001: Changes in cinnamic acid and p-coumaric acid metabolite levels, and transcript levels of the related PPP enzymes in PTI-induced, PIP inhibitor-treated, and control tobacco leaf tissue.

events.event_label: DMSO and PIP reduce, while PTI elicitors increase p-coumaric acid levels
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p087.s001: p-Coumaric acid is directly synthetized from cinnamic acid in plants by C4H. PMC4125134.p087.s002: p-Coumaric acid (Figure 6A) was not detectable in samples inoculated only with DMSO or PIP (negative controls), as opposed to the other samples inoculated with either one of the PTI elicitors P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p087.s003: syringae hrcC- (3.7 nmol/g fresh weight) or flg22 peptide (2.5 nmol/g fresh weight). PMC4125134.p087.s004: However, when PIP was added to the elicitors, concentration of p-coumarate was measured to be less than half at 6 hpi.

events.event_label: chalcone synthase, chalcone isomerase, and PAL are PTI indicator genes in bean
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p093.s001: Tobacco is a well-established model plant for plant-microbe interaction studies, especially in the field of plant-bacterial interactions and a good model plant of the Solanaceae family. PMC4125134.p093.s002: Plant science results should not be generalized incautiously, as differences between plant-groups always occur. PMC4125134.p093.s003: For example in the study of Jakobek and co-workers , bean was used as a model plant and chalcone synthase and chalcone isomerase as indicator genes of PTI, besides a PAL and a chitinase gene. PMC4125134.p093.s004: However the first two indicator genes seem to be specific for leguminous PTI, as they are activated in Medicago as well but not in Arabidopsis and tobacco (Szatmari et al. unpublished).

events.event_label: cinnamic acid is precursor of p-coumaric acid and substrate of C4H in phenylpropanoid pathway
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p083.s001: Thin layer chromatography (TLC) was applied to measure the levels of cinnamic acid, the second phenolic acid in the phenylpropanoid pathway and precursor of p-coumaric acid, moreover substrate of the key rate limiting enzyme of the pathway: C4H (Figure 6A). PMC4125134.p083.s002: Levels of the product, p-coumarate were also measured in tobacco leaf tissue in response to treatments with PTI inducing bacteria (P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p083.s003: syringae hrcC, 108 CFU/ml) and the elicitor flagellin peptide (Flg22, 1 µM), and the C4H inhibitor PIP (1 mM).

events.event_label: PIP alone increases expression of PALa, PALb, C4H, and 4CL
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p090.s006: Interestingly, these 5 genes are the closer ones to phenylalanine, i.e. to the origin of the phenylpropanoid pathway, as can be seen in Figure 4. PMC4125134.p090.s007: Even PIP on its own caused a slight elevation in activities of the first 4 PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL), of which transcription of the C4H gene was most elevated (cca. 3 times).

events.event_label: Phenylalanine conversion to p-coumarate via PAL and C4H
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p111.s006: However, in the case of flg22 peptide treatment only the expected increase in p-coumarate level was observed, while the level of cinnamic acid did not change in these 6 hpi samples as compared to the DMSO control. PMC4125134.p111.s007: A possible explanation to this phenomenon could be that a dynamic equilibrium state of the cinnamate level was maintained here, which would mean that the PAL enzyme could keep up production of cinnamate from phenylalanine at the rate of C4H converting cinnamate to p-coumarate. PMC4125134.p111.s008: In the case of P. syringae hrcC- bacteria a higher level of C4H induction might have occurred, with which PAL could not keep up completely, therefore a relative decrease in cinnamate occurred. PMC4125134.p111.s009: We do not know however if there are other enzymes involved in converting cinnamate into different derivatives, for example into salicylic acid. PMC4125134.p111.s010: Therefore the exact enzymatic mechanisms creating the measured changes in the two phenolic acids will require more studies, for example a higher scale metabolic profiling experiment, preferably including a time series with earlier time points than the 6 hpi samples used here, and/or also applying higher concentrations of flg22.

events.event_label: PIP addition elevates expression of PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL, and OMT-I
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p090.s003: Values in Figure 6B were normalized against actin and water treated control, like in the other RT-PCR measurements. PMC4125134.p090.s004: In general, we saw again that P. s. hrcC- caused stronger activation than flg22. PMC4125134.p090.s005: In the case of 5 out of 7 phenylpropanoid genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL, OMT-I), addition of PIP to the PTI elicitors caused a slight elevation in gene activity. PMC4125134.p090.s006: Interestingly, these 5 genes are the closer ones to phenylalanine, i.e. to the origin of the phenylpropanoid pathway, as can be seen in Figure 4. PMC4125134.p090.s007: Even PIP on its own caused a slight elevation in activities of the first 4 PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL), of which transcription of the C4H gene was most elevated (cca. 3 times).

events.event_label: 1 mM PIP reduces proliferation of P. syringae
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p076.s003: Piperonilic acid (PIP) was chosen, known to be a selective, quasi-irreversible inhibitor of C4H. PMC4125134.p076.s004: Although PIP is toxic to bacteria to a certain extent, it is metabolized fast in planta . PMC4125134.p076.s005: Our in vitro co-incubation experiments showed that proliferation of P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p076.s006: syringae 61 incubated in 1 mM PIP solution for 30 minutes was lowered by two orders of magnitude. PMC4125134.p076.s007: In a dilution series of PIP, 50 µM was the first concentration not to influence bacterial proliferation (data not shown).

PMC4125134.p101.s001: Genes related to formation of cell wall deposits were highly represented in our PTI-related cDNA library. PMC4125134.p101.s002: These were genes of the phenylpropanoid pathway and also some genes of glycine-rich and proline-rich proteins. PMC4125134.p101.s003: Our PTI-activated phenylpropanoid genes covered a major part of the phenylpropanoid pathway (Figure 4). PMC4125134.p101.s004: Real time RT-PCR confirmed both early (6 hpi) and late (48 hpi) activation of these genes (Figure 3B).

events.event_label: PIP inhibits C4H leading to cinnamate accumulation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p112.s001: The basic level of cinnamate increased when PIP was applied. PMC4125134.p112.s002: This could mean that a flow of cinnamate towards another compound(s) is blocked by PIP, therefore cinnamate accumulated. PMC4125134.p112.s003: We know that PIP is a specific inhibitor of C4H therefore we expected this result. PMC4125134.p112.s004: Also, coumarate levels in PTI-induced samples were reduced when PIP was co-infiltrated with either of the two used elicitors.

PMC4125134.p014.s001: Plant cell wall fortification is an important factor of mechanical resistance, the formed papillae are thought to constitute a barrier between the plant cell wall and invading microorganisms. PMC4125134.p014.s002: Papillae are made up of polyphenolics, callose and a network of cell-wall fortifying glycoprotein matrix mainly consisting of glycin-rich proteins and hydroxyproline-rich extensins. PMC4125134.p014.s003: The importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation is well established in plant-fungal interactions. PMC4125134.p014.s004: In the case of bacteria this is speculated to result in a reduced accessibility of nutrients, plus a restricted ability to build up the TTSS and deliver effectors into the plant cell.

PMC4125134.p066.s001: Signal transduction-related genes were selected for further transcriptional investigation as this group of genes might mean a key to the regulation of the development and action of PTI. PMC4125134.p066.s002: The group was highly represented among the identified PTI-related genes, 20 of 176 contigs, i.e. 11% of the contigs. PMC4125134.p066.s003: Members of the best described signal transduction pathway of plant PTI, the MAPK pathway were found: a receptor-kinase (C90) and two MAP-kinases (C24, C58). PMC4125134.p066.s004: Interestingly, contigs of genes representing three calcium-dependent proteins were also found (C53 - calcium-dependent protein kinase 4, C95 - Avr9/Cf-9 rapidly elicited protein 31, C125 - annexin-like protein).

events.event_label: PTI induces phenylpropanoid gene expression in leaves
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p070.s001: Complete network of phenylpropanoid genes is activated in PTI-induced leaves

events.event_label: PIP inhibits C4H activity and PTI development
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p104.s001: Inhibition of C4H activity and PTI development by PIP

events.event_label: PIP affects bacteria and PTI development
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p106.s001: According to our experiments (Information S4) injected PIP seems to have a short inhibitory action on bacteria in planta, but paralelly it seems to have a longer effect on PTI development. PMC4125134.p106.s002: One possible explanation of this would be that PIP, as a quasi-irreversible inhibitor could permanently bind to the active sites of the available cinnamic-acid-4-hydroxylase (C4H) enzymes, thus blocking enzymatic activity for a longer period of time. PMC4125134.p106.s003: It is possible that only the excess free amount of PIP is metabolized soon afterwards. PMC4125134.p106.s004: However, this explanation is hypothetic and requires further studies.

events.event_label: flg22 peptide increases p‑coumarate and does not change cinnamic acid
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p111.s003: Therefore we expected a decrease in cinnamate and a parallel increase in p-coumarate levels in PTI-induced samples. PMC4125134.p111.s004: This exactly occurred in the case of treatment with PTI-inducing P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p111.s005: syringae hrcC- bacteria. PMC4125134.p111.s006: However, in the case of flg22 peptide treatment only the expected increase in p-coumarate level was observed, while the level of cinnamic acid did not change in these 6 hpi samples as compared to the DMSO control. PMC4125134.p111.s007: A possible explanation to this phenomenon could be that a dynamic equilibrium state of the cinnamate level was maintained here, which would mean that the PAL enzyme could keep up production of cinnamate from phenylalanine at the rate of C4H converting cinnamate to p-coumarate.

events.event_label: PIP activates PALa, PALb, C4H and 4CL
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p113.s003: This could be caused by a compensation feedback present in the plant leaves, which senses lack of C4H products in some way. PMC4125134.p113.s004: The first four investigated PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL) were slightly activated upon treatment solely with PIP, and of the four, activation of C4H was highest, about 3-fold. PMC4125134.p113.s005: This might again be result of a feedback loop sensing C4H activity itself, or p-coumarate, or any downstream products. PMC4125134.p113.s006: The actual mechanisms behind these findings require further investigations.

events.event_label: sampling at 6 and 48 hpi in leaves
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p054.s003: Leaves again were infiltrated with 108 CFU/ml suspension of P. syringe hrcC mutant bacteria; however controls were water treated here, as well as in all downstream experiments afterwards. PMC4125134.p054.s004: Leaves were harvested 6 and 48 hpi. PMC4125134.p054.s005: Total RNA was extracted, quantified, transcribed and labelled as described in Materials and Methods. PMC4125134.p054.s006: Hybridization of PTI-induced 6 hpi samples to the cDNA microarrays containing the 176 contigs resulted in 80 detectable contigs (i.e. ratio of medians of pixel intensities significantly divergent from the background, as described in Scanning of Microarrays and Data Analysis in Materials and Methods), of which 52 proved to be activated significantly, i.e. feature signal of the P. syringe hrcC mutant-treated sample was at least 2 times that of the water-treated control, tested by student's T-test on data from three independent replicates (p<0.05), (as shown in Table 1). PMC4125134.p054.s007: The same with 48 hpi samples resulted in 32 detectable contigs, of which 18 were activated significantly.

PMC4125134.p063.s001: Members of some groups of genes were particularly activated in 6 h samples, while this activity was attenuated in 48 h samples. PMC4125134.p063.s002: These so called early gene groups were the auxin, redox-state, signaling, and secondary metabolite related genes, the latter including phenylpropanoid genes.

events.event_label: p‑coumarate levels modulated by PTI elicitors and PIP
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p082.s001: Production of p-coumarate is strongly induced by PTI elicitors, and is inhibited by PIP

events.event_label: Heat‑killed bacteria induce local resistance associated with PTI
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p016.s001: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively. PMC4125134.p016.s002: Later on we found that this local resistance can not only be induced by heat-killed pathogens, but also by saprobic bacteria and some hrp/hrc mutants; i.e. pathogens mutated in their hypersensitivity and pathogenicity genes, many of which code for elements of the Type III Protein Secretion System (T3SS). PMC4125134.p016.s003: Based on our previous findings we regard the local resistance of tobacco as a manifestation of PTI. PMC4125134.p016.s004: We routinely use the HR-inhibiting effect of PTI as a macroscopical demonstration method.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p113.s001: Real time RT-PCR investigation of activity changes of the identified phenylpropanoid genes in response to P. s. syringae hrcC-, flg22 and their combinations with PIP (Figure 6C) showed elevation of gene activities of certain genes in the PIP-combination treatments, as compared to the corresponding elicitor alone-treatment. PMC4125134.p113.s002: These genes were exactly the first five investigated PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL, OMT-I), closest to the origin of the pathway. PMC4125134.p113.s003: This could be caused by a compensation feedback present in the plant leaves, which senses lack of C4H products in some way. PMC4125134.p113.s004: The first four investigated PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL) were slightly activated upon treatment solely with PIP, and of the four, activation of C4H was highest, about 3-fold. PMC4125134.p113.s005: This might again be result of a feedback loop sensing C4H activity itself, or p-coumarate, or any downstream products. PMC4125134.p113.s006: The actual mechanisms behind these findings require further investigations.

PMC4125134.p101.s007: syringae hrcC- bacteria (Figure 3B). PMC4125134.p101.s008: The response to flg22 seemed to fade away earlier as well. PMC4125134.p101.s010: One probable reason for this difference might be that whole bacterial cells contain many other PTI elicitors resulting in a stronger PTI reaction than flagellin alone.

PMC4125134.p077.s011: At the 6-hour challenge treatment the difference in the extent of HR was smaller again between P. s. hrcC and P. s. hrcC+PIP pretreated interveinals, because PTI was already relatively stronger in the hrcC+PIP treated areas.

PMC4125134.p001.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) in Tobacco: Isolation of Activated Genes Suggests Role of the Phenylpropanoid Pathway in Inhibition of Bacterial Pathogens
PMC4125134.p001.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) in Tobacco: Isolation of Activated Genes Suggests Role of the Phenylpropanoid Pathway in Inhibition of Bacterial Pathogens

PMC4125134.p003.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) or Basal Resistance (BR) is a potent, symptomless form of plant resistance. PMC4125134.p003.s002: Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation. PMC4125134.p003.s003: Developed PTI is also able to protect the plant from disease or HR (Hypersensitive Response) after a challenging infection with pathogenic bacteria. PMC4125134.p003.s004: Our aim was to reveal those PTI-related genes of tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) that could possibly play a role in the protection of the plant from disease.

PMC4125134.p003.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) or Basal Resistance (BR) is a potent, symptomless form of plant resistance. PMC4125134.p003.s002: Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation. PMC4125134.p003.s003: Developed PTI is also able to protect the plant from disease or HR (Hypersensitive Response) after a challenging infection with pathogenic bacteria. PMC4125134.p003.s004: Our aim was to reveal those PTI-related genes of tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) that could possibly play a role in the protection of the plant from disease.

PMC4125134.p007.s001: We believe to provide first report on PTI-related changes in the levels of these PPP metabolites. PMC4125134.p007.s002: Results implicated an actual role of the upregulation of the phenylpropanoid pathway in the inhibition of bacterial pathogenic activity during PTI.

PMC4125134.p007.s001: We believe to provide first report on PTI-related changes in the levels of these PPP metabolites. PMC4125134.p007.s002: Results implicated an actual role of the upregulation of the phenylpropanoid pathway in the inhibition of bacterial pathogenic activity during PTI.

PMC4125134.p009.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI), also known as Basal Resistance (BR) is a general form of defense that plants use to maintain their integrity by detecting and eradicating almost any type of invading organism, including non-pathogens. PMC4125134.p009.s002: PTI is symptomless in most cases; however extensive changes occur at the molecular level. PMC4125134.p009.s003: Effector Triggered Immunity (ETI), also known as gene-for-gene resistance of plants, on the other hand requires detection of specific effector proteins of pathogens by plant R-gene products, which eventually leads to the hypersensitive reaction (HR) of plant cells and eradication of that specific pathogen. PMC4125134.p009.s004: This manuscript focuses on molecular changes in plant cells during PTI.

PMC4125134.p009.s004: This manuscript focuses on molecular changes in plant cells during PTI.

PMC4125134.p009.s002: PTI is symptomless in most cases; however extensive changes occur at the molecular level. PMC4125134.p009.s004: This manuscript focuses on molecular changes in plant cells during PTI.

PMC4125134.p011.s001: Several authors studied the transcriptomic changes during PTI, mostly in Arabidopsis. PMC4125134.p011.s002: Navarro et al. treated plants with a fragment of bacterial flagellin (flg22 peptide) and found that the flagellin rapidly elicited (FLARE) genes at 30 and 60 hpi are mainly activated, and only a few are repressed. PMC4125134.p011.s003: Activation is thought to be achieved by derepression, rather than stimulation. PMC4125134.p011.s004: De Torres et al. have found that up to 2 hpi transcriptional changes of HR, PTI, and the compatible relationship are mostly identical.

PMC4125134.p011.s003: Activation is thought to be achieved by derepression, rather than stimulation. PMC4125134.p011.s004: De Torres et al. have found that up to 2 hpi transcriptional changes of HR, PTI, and the compatible relationship are mostly identical. PMC4125134.p011.s005: Truman et al. therefore sought differences of transcriptional patterns at later time points. PMC4125134.p011.s006: At 4 hpi the compatible and hrp mutant bacteria still caused similar changes. PMC4125134.p011.s007: However at 12 hpi, the two transcriptional patterns diverged, so they concluded that effectors of the type-three secretion system (TTSS) and other virulence factors of the compatible pathogen have reprogrammed the sensitive plant cell by this time point.

PMC4125134.p012.s003: They include flagellin, LPS (lipopolysaccharide,), cold shock protein and elongation factor Tu. PMC4125134.p012.s004: Identification of the flagellin receptor FLS2 opened the way to determination of downstream signaling events. PMC4125134.p012.s005: FLS2 is a leucine-rich repeat receptor-like kinase just like the receptor of elongation factor Tu.

events.event_label: cell wall fortification contributes_to plant-fungal interactions
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p014.s001: Plant cell wall fortification is an important factor of mechanical resistance, the formed papillae are thought to constitute a barrier between the plant cell wall and invading microorganisms. PMC4125134.p014.s002: Papillae are made up of polyphenolics, callose and a network of cell-wall fortifying glycoprotein matrix mainly consisting of glycin-rich proteins and hydroxyproline-rich extensins. PMC4125134.p014.s003: The importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation is well established in plant-fungal interactions. PMC4125134.p014.s004: In the case of bacteria this is speculated to result in a reduced accessibility of nutrients, plus a restricted ability to build up the TTSS and deliver effectors into the plant cell.

PMC4125134.p014.s006: tomato DC3000 to suppress activation of plant cell wall genes. PMC4125134.p014.s007: Hauck et al. found that AvrPto is an effector that suppresses cell-wall based defense in PTI. PMC4125134.p014.s008: This was supported by reduced callose staining - an indicator of papilla formation - in Avr Pto transgenic Arabidopsis plants.

PMC4125134.p014.s006: tomato DC3000 to suppress activation of plant cell wall genes. PMC4125134.p014.s008: This was supported by reduced callose staining - an indicator of papilla formation - in Avr Pto transgenic Arabidopsis plants.

PMC4125134.p015.s001: The phenylpropanoid biosynthetic pathway (PPP) could promote cell wall fortification by lignin synthesis, but it is also an important source of compounds with direct antimicrobial activities. PMC4125134.p015.s002: It is not known, whether it plays a role in PTI against bacteria, but its importance in different plant-fungal interactions has long been shown. PMC4125134.p015.s003: Tobacco plants with suppressed PAL activity - the second enzyme of PPP - have been shown to be more susceptible to the compatible pathogen Cercospora nicotianae .

PMC4125134.p018.s001: In this study we sought to better understand the molecular mechanisms underlying the physiological and biochemical processes of tobacco PTI. PMC4125134.p018.s002: Especially the ones leading to inhibition of bacteria, as the exact way by which the plant achieves symptomless arrest of bacterial growth is yet unknown. PMC4125134.p018.s003: At first we identified over 400 EST-s representing genes that are activated during PTI using suppression subtractive hybridization (SSH). PMC4125134.p018.s004: Following assembly of the EST sequences into 176 contigs, their transcriptional activation was confirmed by cDNA microarray and real time RT-PCR.

PMC4125134.p018.s003: At first we identified over 400 EST-s representing genes that are activated during PTI using suppression subtractive hybridization (SSH). PMC4125134.p018.s004: Following assembly of the EST sequences into 176 contigs, their transcriptional activation was confirmed by cDNA microarray and real time RT-PCR. PMC4125134.p018.s005: A highly represented group in our library, that of the phenylpropanoid genes, was strongly implicated to play an important role in mediating the antimicrobial effects of PTI, as shown by transcriptional (microarray and RT-PCR) data, as well as HR-inhibition assays using pharmacological inhibition of a key enzyme of the phenylpropanoid pathway.
PMC4125134.p047.s001: 176 putatively PTI-activated tobacco genes were found by suppression subtractive hybridization

PMC4125134.p066.s001: Signal transduction-related genes were selected for further transcriptional investigation as this group of genes might mean a key to the regulation of the development and action of PTI. PMC4125134.p066.s002: The group was highly represented among the identified PTI-related genes, 20 of 176 contigs, i.e. 11% of the contigs. PMC4125134.p066.s003: Members of the best described signal transduction pathway of plant PTI, the MAPK pathway were found: a receptor-kinase (C90) and two MAP-kinases (C24, C58). PMC4125134.p066.s004: Interestingly, contigs of genes representing three calcium-dependent proteins were also found (C53 - calcium-dependent protein kinase 4, C95 - Avr9/Cf-9 rapidly elicited protein 31, C125 - annexin-like protein).

PMC4125134.p076.s006: syringae 61 incubated in 1 mM PIP solution for 30 minutes was lowered by two orders of magnitude. PMC4125134.p076.s007: In a dilution series of PIP, 50 µM was the first concentration not to influence bacterial proliferation (data not shown). PMC4125134.p076.s008: We tested if these characteristics could affect our planned experiments and found that it had no adverse effects on HR-development if bacteria were injected at least 2 hours after PIP administration (Information S4 A). PMC4125134.p076.s009: Only when the incompatible P. syringae pv. syringae 61 was co-infiltrated with PIP (i.e. 0 hpi) did we detect a lack of HR, possibly because of inhibition of bacterial activity by PIP. PMC4125134.p076.s010: Therefore after considering several possible C4H inhibitors from literature, PIP seemed to be the most suitable for our experiments because of its selective and quasi-irreversible nature.

PMC4125134.p080.s006: Results were consistent with the HR-inhibition tests. PMC4125134.p080.s007: Conductivity (corresponding to electrolyte leakage and HR) was higher both in the 5-hour and the 6-hour P. s. hrcC+PIP samples. PMC4125134.p080.s008: The difference between the two pretreatments was greater in the 5-hour samples, just as seen in Figure 5B.

PMC4125134.p081.s001: To rule out the possibility that a direct inhibitory action of PIP on the P. syringae pv. syringae hrcC bacteria used for pretreatment caused the detected difference in the extent of PTI development, we repeated the HR-inhibition experiment with heat-killed P. s. hrcC and heat-killed P. s. hrcC+PIP as pretreatments. PMC4125134.p081.s002: Results were similar with heat-killed as with live P. s. hrcC pretreatments, i.e. addition of PIP caused diminished HR upon challenge inoculation with incompatible strain P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p081.s003: syringae 61 (representative leaf image: Information S4 B).

PMC4125134.p086.s006: Looking at pairs of treatment X and treatment X+PIP, the largest difference was found between hrcC and hrcC+PIP treatment among our experimental settings, and difference between flg22 and flg22+PIP was also significant. PMC4125134.p086.s007: Knowing that PAL, the previous enzyme in the phenylpropanoid pathway is also activated upon PTI elicitor treatment, one might hypothesize that when elicitors are added, PAL creates elevated levels of cinnamate, which is then normally turned into coumarate by C4H. PMC4125134.p086.s008: This transformation is then stuck by the inhibitor PIP, resulting in high levels of cinnamate.

PMC4125134.p089.s003: We would have liked to see how changes in transcriptional levels relate to changes in metabolite levels in Figure 6A. PMC4125134.p089.s004: We expected that if PIP is supposed to inhibit PTI through inhibiting C4H, than the plant might try to counteract its effect on a transcriptional level. PMC4125134.p089.s005: This would mean that PIP addition would elevate transcriptional levels of some PTI-related PPP genes, or at least of C4H.

PMC4125134.p097.s001: MAPK and calcium signaling related genes in the PTI library

PMC4125134.p098.s001: Unraveling the signaling pathways involved in PTI would bring us closer to the possibility of breeding or engineering plants with a higher capacity of PTI. PMC4125134.p098.s002: Some authors have already made important steps towards this task. PMC4125134.p098.s003: Asai et al. followed a complete MAPK cascade in Arabidopsis from the perception of the MAMP flagellin by its receptor-like-kinase-type receptor FLS2 to the corresponding transcription factors. PMC4125134.p098.s004: However, genetic evidence later showed that the first element, MEKK1 in fact belongs to another module: MEKK1-MKK1-MPK4.

PMC4125134.p099.s001: Another group of our activated signaling genes during PTI were related to Ca2+ mediated signaling. PMC4125134.p099.s002: Only a few examples of MAMP-induced elevation of intracellular Ca2+ levels exist in plants so far. PMC4125134.p099.s003: De Torres et al. showed that a sustained elevation of plant intracellular Ca2+ levels occurs in an apparently avr-dependent manner. PMC4125134.p099.s004: Earlier work from the same laboratory however showed the existence of an early peak of intracellular Ca2+ level, which was triggered by bacteria mutant in the type III secretion system (PTI-inducing) as well as incompatible strains.

PMC4125134.p099.s001: Another group of our activated signaling genes during PTI were related to Ca2+ mediated signaling. PMC4125134.p099.s002: Only a few examples of MAMP-induced elevation of intracellular Ca2+ levels exist in plants so far. PMC4125134.p099.s003: De Torres et al. showed that a sustained elevation of plant intracellular Ca2+ levels occurs in an apparently avr-dependent manner. PMC4125134.p099.s004: Earlier work from the same laboratory however showed the existence of an early peak of intracellular Ca2+ level, which was triggered by bacteria mutant in the type III secretion system (PTI-inducing) as well as incompatible strains.

PMC4125134.p103.s001: It was interesting that genes of reactions leading to lignin formation were overrepresented while branches to salicylic acid, flavonoids, anthocyanins, and stilbenes were not present at all (Figure 4). PMC4125134.p103.s002: This was not unexpected based on our and others' findings that salicylic acid (SA) is not an important factor of local PTI in tobacco. PMC4125134.p103.s003: The innovative work of Huang et al. showed the lack of SA accumulation after inoculation of tobacco with P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p103.s004: tomato DC3000 hrcC− mutant using a SA biosensor.

PMC4125134.p105.s001: We applied PIP, a potent selective inhibitor of C4H – which is an early rate-limiting enzyme of the phenylpropanoid pathway – to investigate if disturbing the pathway at a key step has any effect on the outcome of PTI induction (Figure 5).

PMC4125134.p107.s001: Our HR-inhibition test results indicated that a significant decrease in the HR-inhibiting ability of PTI-inducing bacteria occurred (Figure 5A, 5B). PMC4125134.p107.s002: This means that inhibition of C4H by PIP inhibited PTI as well to a certain extent, determined by our experiments. PMC4125134.p107.s003: Thus, disturbing the phenylpropanoid pathway at an early stage had an adverse effect on the efficiency of PTI. PMC4125134.p107.s004: Visual evaluation of the HR-inhibition tests was also supported by electrolyte leakage measurements, where elevations in conductivity are related to the degree of HR necrosis.

PMC4125134.p107.s003: Thus, disturbing the phenylpropanoid pathway at an early stage had an adverse effect on the efficiency of PTI. PMC4125134.p107.s004: Visual evaluation of the HR-inhibition tests was also supported by electrolyte leakage measurements, where elevations in conductivity are related to the degree of HR necrosis.
PMC4125134.p110.s001: p-Coumarate formation is linked to PTI elicitation in tobacco
PMC4125134.p110.s001: p-Coumarate formation is linked to PTI elicitation in tobacco

PMC4125134.p111.s001: To assess, if the actual flux of phenolic compounds follows our assumptions based on HR-inhibition tests and transcriptional data, we quantified cinnamate and p-coumarate, the second and third phenolic compounds in the phenylpropanoid pathway (as cinnamic and p-coumaric acids) by the use of a TLC-densitometry method (Figure 6A and B). PMC4125134.p111.s002: Transcriptional and HR-inhibition test data implied that C4H activity should be enhanced during PTI. PMC4125134.p111.s003: Therefore we expected a decrease in cinnamate and a parallel increase in p-coumarate levels in PTI-induced samples. PMC4125134.p111.s004: This exactly occurred in the case of treatment with PTI-inducing P. syringae pv.

PMC4125134.p116.s001: Based on transcriptional activation, HR-inhibition tests, electrolyte leakage experiments, and TLC results we concluded that C4H seems to play an important role in the development of PTI. PMC4125134.p116.s002: This however raises a lot of further questions to be answered in the future. PMC4125134.p116.s003: We are planning to develop a HPLC based method to measure changes of phenolic compounds at a higher scale, to better understand the roles of these compounds in PTI. PMC4125134.p116.s004: We are especially curious about the changes in the levels of phenylalanine, salicylic acid, other phenolic acids and alcohols, flavonoids etc.

PMC4125134.p117.s001: Investigating the role of PPP in PTI, one shall remember that the presumably formed phenolic acids (i.e. coumarate, caffeate, ferulate) have been shown to have direct antimicrobial activity, which might also add to the efficiency of PTI. PMC4125134.p117.s002: Importance of the antioxidant activity of phenylpropanoid products has been shown by Kostyn et al. in the plant–fungus interaction context. PMC4125134.p117.s003: The detected transcriptional activation of flax phenylpropanoid genes in response to Fusarium infection was supported by metabolite profiling. PMC4125134.p117.s004: Elevated antioxidant status of infected plants was verified by measuring ROS (Reactive Oxygen Species) scavenging activities of identified compounds.

relations.subject: PTI

evidence.text: Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation.

relations.evidence_preview: Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p003.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) or Basal Resistance (BR) is a potent, symptomless form of plant resistance. PMC4125134.p003.s002: Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation. PMC4125134.p003.s003: Developed PTI is also able to protect the plant from disease or HR (Hypersensitive Response) after a challenging infection with pathogenic bacteria. PMC4125134.p003.s004: Our aim was to reveal those PTI-related genes of tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) that could possibly play a role in the protection of the plant from disease.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) or Basal Resistance (BR) is a potent, symptomless form of plant resistance.
Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation.
Developed PTI is also able to protect the plant from disease or HR (Hypersensitive Response) after a challenging infection with pathogenic bacteria.
Our aim was to reveal those PTI-related genes of tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) that could possibly play a role in the protection of the plant from disease.

relations.subject: PTI

evidence.text: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI), also known as Basal Resistance (BR) is a general form of defense that plants use to maintain their integrity by detecting and eradicating almost any type of invading organism, including non-pathogens.

relations.evidence_preview: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI), also known as Basal Resistance (BR) is a general form of defense that plants use to maintain their integrity by detecting and eradicating almost any type of invading organism, including non-pathogens.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p009.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI), also known as Basal Resistance (BR) is a general form of defense that plants use to maintain their integrity by detecting and eradicating almost any type of invading organism, including non-pathogens. PMC4125134.p009.s002: PTI is symptomless in most cases; however extensive changes occur at the molecular level. PMC4125134.p009.s003: Effector Triggered Immunity (ETI), also known as gene-for-gene resistance of plants, on the other hand requires detection of specific effector proteins of pathogens by plant R-gene products, which eventually leads to the hypersensitive reaction (HR) of plant cells and eradication of that specific pathogen. PMC4125134.p009.s004: This manuscript focuses on molecular changes in plant cells during PTI.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI), also known as Basal Resistance (BR) is a general form of defense that plants use to maintain their integrity by detecting and eradicating almost any type of invading organism, including non-pathogens.
PTI is symptomless in most cases; however extensive changes occur at the molecular level.

Effector Triggered Immunity (ETI), also known as gene-for-gene resistance of plants, on the other hand requires detection of specific effector proteins of pathogens by plant R-gene products, which eventually leads to the hypersensitive reaction (HR) of plant cells and eradication of that specific pathogen.
This manuscript focuses on molecular changes in plant cells during PTI.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI), also known as Basal Resistance (BR) is a general form of defense that plants use to maintain their integrity by detecting and eradicating almost any type of invading organism, including non-pathogens.
PTI is symptomless in most cases; however extensive changes occur at the molecular level.

Effector Triggered Immunity (ETI), also known as gene-for-gene resistance of plants, on the other hand requires detection of specific effector proteins of pathogens by plant R-gene products, which eventually leads to the hypersensitive reaction (HR) of plant cells and eradication of that specific pathogen.
This manuscript focuses on molecular changes in plant cells during PTI.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Several authors studied the transcriptomic changes during PTI, mostly in Arabidopsis.

Navarro et al. treated plants with a fragment of bacterial flagellin (flg22 peptide) and found that the flagellin rapidly elicited (FLARE) genes at 30 and 60 hpi are mainly activated, and only a few are repressed. Activation is thought to be achieved by derepression, rather than stimulation.

De Torres et al. have found that up to 2 hpi transcriptional changes of HR, PTI, and the compatible relationship are mostly identical. Truman et al. therefore sought differences of transcriptional patterns at later time points.
At 4 hpi the compatible and hrp mutant bacteria still caused similar changes.

PMC4125134.p011.s007: However at 12 hpi, the two transcriptional patterns diverged, so they concluded that effectors of the type-three secretion system (TTSS) and other virulence factors of the compatible pathogen have reprogrammed the sensitive plant cell by this time point.

evidence_context_sentences.text: De Torres et al. have found that up to 2 hpi transcriptional changes of HR, PTI, and the compatible relationship are mostly identical. Truman et al. therefore sought differences of transcriptional patterns at later time points.
At 4 hpi the compatible and hrp mutant bacteria still caused similar changes.

However at 12 hpi, the two transcriptional patterns diverged, so they concluded that effectors of the type-three secretion system (TTSS) and other virulence factors of the compatible pathogen have reprogrammed the sensitive plant cell by this time point.
At 4 hpi the compatible and hrp mutant bacteria still caused similar changes.

However at 12 hpi, the two transcriptional patterns diverged, so they concluded that effectors of the type-three secretion system (TTSS) and other virulence factors of the compatible pathogen have reprogrammed the sensitive plant cell by this time point.

relations.subject: callose

evidence.text: Papillae are made up of polyphenolics, callose and a network of cell-wall fortifying glycoprotein matrix mainly consisting of glycin-rich proteins and hydroxyproline-rich extensins.

PMC4125134.p014.s003: The importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation is well established in plant-fungal interactions. evidence_context_sentences.text: Plant cell wall fortification is an important factor of mechanical resistance, the formed papillae are thought to constitute a barrier between the plant cell wall and invading microorganisms. Papillae are made up of polyphenolics, callose and a network of cell-wall fortifying glycoprotein matrix mainly consisting of glycin-rich proteins and hydroxyproline-rich extensins.
The importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation is well established in plant-fungal interactions.
In the case of bacteria this is speculated to result in a reduced accessibility of nutrients, plus a restricted ability to build up the TTSS and deliver effectors into the plant cell.

The possible importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation in PTI is supported by transcriptional evidence that showed the ability of the compatible Arabidopsis pathogen Pseudomonas syringae pv.

relations.triple: heat-killed pathogenic bacteria activates_or_induces local resistance
relations.subject: heat-killed pathogenic bacteria

evidence.text: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively.

relations.evidence_preview: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p016.s001: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively. PMC4125134.p016.s002: Later on we found that this local resistance can not only be induced by heat-killed pathogens, but also by saprobic bacteria and some hrp/hrc mutants; i.e. pathogens mutated in their hypersensitivity and pathogenicity genes, many of which code for elements of the Type III Protein Secretion System (T3SS). PMC4125134.p016.s003: Based on our previous findings we regard the local resistance of tobacco as a manifestation of PTI. PMC4125134.p016.s004: We routinely use the HR-inhibiting effect of PTI as a macroscopical demonstration method.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively.
Later on we found that this local resistance can not only be induced by heat-killed pathogens, but also by saprobic bacteria and some hrp/hrc mutants; i.
e. pathogens mutated in their hypersensitivity and pathogenicity genes, many of which code for elements of the Type III Protein Secretion System (T3SS).
Based on our previous findings we regard the local resistance of tobacco as a manifestation of PTI.

relations.subject: hrp gene

evidence.text: Hrp/hrc mutants of the Pseudomonas syringae pv. syringae 61 bacterial strain (original wild type causing brown spot on bean and HR on tobacco) are damaged in their ability to cause disease on bean or HR on tobacco to varying extent, depending on which hrp gene is turned off.

relations.subject: PTI

evidence.text: At first we identified over 400 EST-s representing genes that are activated during PTI using suppression subtractive hybridization (SSH).

relations.evidence_preview: At first we identified over 400 EST-s representing genes that are activated during PTI using suppression subtractive hybridization (SSH).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p018.s001: In this study we sought to better understand the molecular mechanisms underlying the physiological and biochemical processes of tobacco PTI. PMC4125134.p018.s002: Especially the ones leading to inhibition of bacteria, as the exact way by which the plant achieves symptomless arrest of bacterial growth is yet unknown. PMC4125134.p018.s003: At first we identified over 400 EST-s representing genes that are activated during PTI using suppression subtractive hybridization (SSH). PMC4125134.p018.s004: Following assembly of the EST sequences into 176 contigs, their transcriptional activation was confirmed by cDNA microarray and real time RT-PCR.

evidence_context_sentences.text: In this study we sought to better understand the molecular mechanisms underlying the physiological and biochemical processes of tobacco PTI.
Especially the ones leading to inhibition of bacteria, as the exact way by which the plant achieves symptomless arrest of bacterial growth is yet unknown.
At first we identified over 400 EST-s representing genes that are activated during PTI using suppression subtractive hybridization (SSH).
Following assembly of the EST sequences into 176 contigs, their transcriptional activation was confirmed by cDNA microarray and real time RT-PCR.

relations.subject: phenylpropanoid genes

evidence.text: A highly represented group in our library, that of the phenylpropanoid genes, was strongly implicated to play an important role in mediating the antimicrobial effects of PTI, as shown by transcriptional (microarray and RT-PCR) data, as well as HR-inhibition assays using pharmacological inhibition of a key enzyme of the phenylpropanoid pathway.

relations.subject: 6 hpi

evidence.text: Leaves were harvested 6 and 48 hpi.

relations.evidence_preview: Leaves were harvested 6 and 48 hpi.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p054.s003: Leaves again were infiltrated with 108 CFU/ml suspension of P. syringe hrcC mutant bacteria; however controls were water treated here, as well as in all downstream experiments afterwards. PMC4125134.p054.s004: Leaves were harvested 6 and 48 hpi. PMC4125134.p054.s005: Total RNA was extracted, quantified, transcribed and labelled as described in Materials and Methods. PMC4125134.p054.s006: Hybridization of PTI-induced 6 hpi samples to the cDNA microarrays containing the 176 contigs resulted in 80 detectable contigs (i.e. ratio of medians of pixel intensities significantly divergent from the background, as described in Scanning of Microarrays and Data Analysis in Materials and Methods), of which 52 proved to be activated significantly, i.e. feature signal of the P. syringe hrcC mutant-treated sample was at least 2 times that of the water-treated control, tested by student's T-test on data from three independent replicates (p<0.05), (as shown in Table 1). PMC4125134.p054.s007: The same with 48 hpi samples resulted in 32 detectable contigs, of which 18 were activated significantly.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Leaves again were infiltrated with 108 CFU/ml suspension of P.
syringe hrcC mutant bacteria; however controls were water treated here, as well as in all downstream experiments afterwards.
Leaves were harvested 6 and 48 hpi.
Total RNA was extracted, quantified, transcribed and labelled as described in Materials and Methods.
Hybridization of PTI-induced 6 hpi samples to the cDNA microarrays containing the 176 contigs resulted in 80 detectable contigs (i.

relations.subject: 6 h

evidence.text: Members of some groups of genes were particularly activated in 6 h samples, while this activity was attenuated in 48 h samples.
These so called early gene groups were the auxin, redox-state, signaling, and secondary metabolite related genes, the latter including phenylpropanoid genes.

relations.subject: Signal transduction-related genes

evidence.text: The group was highly represented among the identified PTI-related genes, 20 of 176 contigs, i.e. 11% of the contigs.

relations.evidence_preview: The group was highly represented among the identified PTI-related genes, 20 of 176 contigs, i.e. 11% of the contigs.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p066.s001: Signal transduction-related genes were selected for further transcriptional investigation as this group of genes might mean a key to the regulation of the development and action of PTI. PMC4125134.p066.s002: The group was highly represented among the identified PTI-related genes, 20 of 176 contigs, i.e. 11% of the contigs. PMC4125134.p066.s003: Members of the best described signal transduction pathway of plant PTI, the MAPK pathway were found: a receptor-kinase (C90) and two MAP-kinases (C24, C58). PMC4125134.p066.s004: Interestingly, contigs of genes representing three calcium-dependent proteins were also found (C53 - calcium-dependent protein kinase 4, C95 - Avr9/Cf-9 rapidly elicited protein 31, C125 - annexin-like protein).

relations.subject: PIP

evidence.text: Although PIP is toxic to bacteria to a certain extent, it is metabolized fast in planta .

relations.evidence_preview: Although PIP is toxic to bacteria to a certain extent, it is metabolized fast in planta .
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p076.s003: Piperonilic acid (PIP) was chosen, known to be a selective, quasi-irreversible inhibitor of C4H. PMC4125134.p076.s004: Although PIP is toxic to bacteria to a certain extent, it is metabolized fast in planta . PMC4125134.p076.s005: Our in vitro co-incubation experiments showed that proliferation of P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p076.s006: syringae 61 incubated in 1 mM PIP solution for 30 minutes was lowered by two orders of magnitude. PMC4125134.p076.s007: In a dilution series of PIP, 50 µM was the first concentration not to influence bacterial proliferation (data not shown).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Piperonilic acid (PIP) was chosen, known to be a selective, quasi-irreversible inhibitor of C4H.
Although PIP is toxic to bacteria to a certain extent, it is metabolized fast in planta .
Our in vitro co-incubation experiments showed that proliferation of P.
syringae pv.
syringae 61 incubated in 1 mM PIP solution for 30 minutes was lowered by two orders of magnitude.

relations.subject: PTI

evidence.text: Knowing that PAL, the previous enzyme in the phenylpropanoid pathway is also activated upon PTI elicitor treatment, one might hypothesize that when elicitors are added, PAL creates elevated levels of cinnamate, which is then normally turned into coumarate by C4H.

relations.evidence_preview: Knowing that PAL, the previous enzyme in the phenylpropanoid pathway is also activated upon PTI elicitor treatment, one might hypothesize that when elicitors are added, PAL creates elevated levels of cinnamate, which is then normally turned into coumarate by C4H.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p086.s006: Looking at pairs of treatment X and treatment X+PIP, the largest difference was found between hrcC and hrcC+PIP treatment among our experimental settings, and difference between flg22 and flg22+PIP was also significant. PMC4125134.p086.s007: Knowing that PAL, the previous enzyme in the phenylpropanoid pathway is also activated upon PTI elicitor treatment, one might hypothesize that when elicitors are added, PAL creates elevated levels of cinnamate, which is then normally turned into coumarate by C4H. PMC4125134.p086.s008: This transformation is then stuck by the inhibitor PIP, resulting in high levels of cinnamate.

evidence_context_sentences.text: syringae pv.

syringae hrcC-) alone caused significant decrease in cinnamate levels (about halved the concentration) as expected, while treatment with flg22 peptide maintained cinnamate quantity at the basic level of 11 nmol/g fresh weight in the measured 6 hpi samples.

Looking at pairs of treatment X and treatment X+PIP, the largest difference was found between hrcC and hrcC+PIP treatment among our experimental settings, and difference between flg22 and flg22+PIP was also significant.

evidence_context_sentences.text: We would have liked to see how changes in transcriptional levels relate to changes in metabolite levels in Figure 6A.
We expected that if PIP is supposed to inhibit PTI through inhibiting C4H, than the plant might try to counteract its effect on a transcriptional level.
This would mean that PIP addition would elevate transcriptional levels of some PTI-related PPP genes, or at least of C4H.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Tobacco is a well-established model plant for plant-microbe interaction studies, especially in the field of plant-bacterial interactions and a good model plant of the Solanaceae family.
Plant science results should not be generalized incautiously, as differences between plant-groups always occur.
For example in the study of Jakobek and co-workers  , bean was used as a model plant and chalcone synthase and chalcone isomerase as indicator genes of PTI, besides a PAL and a chitinase gene.
However the first two indicator genes seem to be specific for leguminous PTI, as they are activated in Medicago as well but not in Arabidopsis  and tobacco (Szatmari et al. unpublished).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Unraveling the signaling pathways involved in PTI would bring us closer to the possibility of breeding or engineering plants with a higher capacity of PTI.
Some authors have already made important steps towards this task.
Asai et al. followed a complete MAPK cascade in Arabidopsis from the perception of the MAMP flagellin by its receptor-like-kinase-type receptor FLS2 to the corresponding transcription factors.
However, genetic evidence later showed that the first element, MEKK1 in fact belongs to another module: MEKK1-MKK1-MPK4.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Another group of our activated signaling genes during PTI were related to Ca2+ mediated signaling.
Only a few examples of MAMP-induced elevation of intracellular Ca2+ levels exist in plants so far.
De Torres et al. showed that a sustained elevation of plant intracellular Ca2+ levels occurs in an apparently avr-dependent manner.

Earlier work from the same laboratory however showed the existence of an early peak of intracellular Ca2+ level, which was triggered by bacteria mutant in the type III secretion system (PTI-inducing) as well as incompatible strains.

PMC4125134.p106.s002: One possible explanation of this would be that PIP, as a quasi-irreversible inhibitor could permanently bind to the active sites of the available cinnamic-acid-4-hydroxylase (C4H) enzymes, thus blocking enzymatic activity for a longer period of time. PMC4125134.p106.s003: It is possible that only the excess free amount of PIP is metabolized soon afterwards. evidence_context_sentences.text: According to our experiments (Information S4) injected PIP seems to have a short inhibitory action on bacteria in planta, but paralelly it seems to have a longer effect on PTI development.

One possible explanation of this would be that PIP, as a quasi-irreversible inhibitor could permanently bind to the active sites of the available cinnamic-acid-4-hydroxylase (C4H) enzymes, thus blocking enzymatic activity for a longer period of time.
It is possible that only the excess free amount of PIP is metabolized soon afterwards.
However, this explanation is hypothetic and requires further studies.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p107.s001: Our HR-inhibition test results indicated that a significant decrease in the HR-inhibiting ability of PTI-inducing bacteria occurred (Figure 5A, 5B). PMC4125134.p107.s002: This means that inhibition of C4H by PIP inhibited PTI as well to a certain extent, determined by our experiments. PMC4125134.p107.s003: Thus, disturbing the phenylpropanoid pathway at an early stage had an adverse effect on the efficiency of PTI. PMC4125134.p107.s004: Visual evaluation of the HR-inhibition tests was also supported by electrolyte leakage measurements, where elevations in conductivity are related to the degree of HR necrosis.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Our HR-inhibition test results indicated that a significant decrease in the HR-inhibiting ability of PTI-inducing bacteria occurred (Figure 5A, 5B).
This means that inhibition of C4H by PIP inhibited PTI as well to a certain extent, determined by our experiments.
Thus, disturbing the phenylpropanoid pathway at an early stage had an adverse effect on the efficiency of PTI.
Visual evaluation of the HR-inhibition tests was also supported by electrolyte leakage measurements, where elevations in conductivity are related to the degree of HR necrosis.

syringae hrcC- bacteria a higher level of C4H induction might have occurred, with which PAL could not keep up completely, therefore a relative decrease in cinnamate occurred. evidence_context_sentences.text: The basic level of cinnamate increased when PIP was applied. This could mean that a flow of cinnamate towards another compound(s) is blocked by PIP, therefore cinnamate accumulated. We know that PIP is a specific inhibitor of C4H therefore we expected this result.
Also, coumarate levels in PTI-induced samples were reduced when PIP was co-infiltrated with either of the two used elicitors.

evidence.text: Real time RT-PCR investigation of activity changes of the identified phenylpropanoid genes in response to P. s. syringae hrcC-, flg22 and their combinations with PIP (Figure 6C) showed elevation of gene activities of certain genes in the PIP-combination treatments, as compared to the corresponding elicitor alone-treatment.

relations.subject: C4H

evidence.text: Based on transcriptional activation, HR-inhibition tests, electrolyte leakage experiments, and TLC results we concluded that C4H seems to play an important role in the development of PTI.

relations.evidence_preview: Based on transcriptional activation, HR-inhibition tests, electrolyte leakage experiments, and TLC results we concluded that C4H seems to play an important role in the development of PTI.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p116.s001: Based on transcriptional activation, HR-inhibition tests, electrolyte leakage experiments, and TLC results we concluded that C4H seems to play an important role in the development of PTI. PMC4125134.p116.s002: This however raises a lot of further questions to be answered in the future. PMC4125134.p116.s003: We are planning to develop a HPLC based method to measure changes of phenolic compounds at a higher scale, to better understand the roles of these compounds in PTI. PMC4125134.p116.s004: We are especially curious about the changes in the levels of phenylalanine, salicylic acid, other phenolic acids and alcohols, flavonoids etc.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Based on transcriptional activation, HR-inhibition tests, electrolyte leakage experiments, and TLC results we concluded that C4H seems to play an important role in the development of PTI.
This however raises a lot of further questions to be answered in the future.
We are planning to develop a HPLC based method to measure changes of phenolic compounds at a higher scale, to better understand the roles of these compounds in PTI.

We are especially curious about the changes in the levels of phenylalanine, salicylic acid, other phenolic acids and alcohols, flavonoids etc. We are also planning VIGS of the phenylpropanoid genes investigated here, to test the effect of their inhibition on the development of PTI.

entities.selected_label: effector-mediated activation of plant hypersensitive response by symbiont
entities.selected_description: A symbiont process in which a molecule secreted by the symbiont activates a resistance gene-dependent defense response signaling pathway in the plant host, in order to activate a hypersensitive response to induce necrosis. In the plant, this process involves the direct or indirect recognition of the symbiont effector protein for example through plant resistance receptor or R proteins (or R genes).

entities.selected_description: An intracellular protein kinase cascade containing at least a MAP kinase (MAPK). It starts with the activation of a MAP3K, and the consecutive activation of a MPK2K and a MAPK. The cascade can also contain an additional tier: the upstream MAP4K. The kinases in each tier phosphorylate and activate the kinase in the downstream tier to transmit a signal within a cell.

sentences.text: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) in Tobacco: Isolation of Activated Genes Suggests Role of the Phenylpropanoid Pathway in Inhibition of Bacterial Pathogens
Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) or Basal Resistance (BR) is a potent, symptomless form of plant resistance.
Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation.
Developed PTI is also able to protect the plant from disease or HR (Hypersensitive Response) after a challenging infection with pathogenic bacteria.
Our aim was to reveal those PTI-related genes of tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) that could possibly play a role in the protection of the plant from disease.
We believe to provide first report on PTI-related changes in the levels of these PPP metabolites.
Results implicated an actual role of the upregulation of the phenylpropanoid pathway in the inhibition of bacterial pathogenic activity during PTI.

Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI), also known as Basal Resistance (BR) is a general form of defense that plants use to maintain their integrity by detecting and eradicating almost any type of invading organism, including non-pathogens.
PTI is symptomless in most cases; however extensive changes occur at the molecular level.

Effector Triggered Immunity (ETI), also known as gene-for-gene resistance of plants, on the other hand requires detection of specific effector proteins of pathogens by plant R-gene products, which eventually leads to the hypersensitive reaction (HR) of plant cells and eradication of that specific pathogen.
This manuscript focuses on molecular changes in plant cells during PTI.
Several authors studied the transcriptomic changes during PTI, mostly in Arabidopsis.

Navarro et al. treated plants with a fragment of bacterial flagellin (flg22 peptide) and found that the flagellin rapidly elicited (FLARE) genes at 30 and 60 hpi are mainly activated, and only a few are repressed. Activation is thought to be achieved by derepression, rather than stimulation.

De Torres et al. have found that up to 2 hpi transcriptional changes of HR, PTI, and the compatible relationship are mostly identical. Truman et al. therefore sought differences of transcriptional patterns at later time points.
At 4 hpi the compatible and hrp mutant bacteria still caused similar changes.

However at 12 hpi, the two transcriptional patterns diverged, so they concluded that effectors of the type-three secretion system (TTSS) and other virulence factors of the compatible pathogen have reprogrammed the sensitive plant cell by this time point.
They include flagellin, LPS (lipopolysaccharide,), cold shock protein and elongation factor Tu.
Identification of the flagellin receptor FLS2 opened the way to determination of downstream signaling events.
FLS2 is a leucine-rich repeat receptor-like kinase just like the receptor of elongation factor Tu.

Plant cell wall fortification is an important factor of mechanical resistance, the formed papillae are thought to constitute a barrier between the plant cell wall and invading microorganisms. Papillae are made up of polyphenolics, callose and a network of cell-wall fortifying glycoprotein matrix mainly consisting of glycin-rich proteins and hydroxyproline-rich extensins.
The importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation is well established in plant-fungal interactions.
In the case of bacteria this is speculated to result in a reduced accessibility of nutrients, plus a restricted ability to build up the TTSS and deliver effectors into the plant cell.

The possible importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation in PTI is supported by transcriptional evidence that showed the ability of the compatible Arabidopsis pathogen Pseudomonas syringae pv.
Hauck et al. found that AvrPto is an effector that suppresses cell-wall based defense in PTI.
This was supported by reduced callose staining - an indicator of papilla formation - in Avr Pto transgenic Arabidopsis plants.
The phenylpropanoid biosynthetic pathway (PPP) could promote cell wall fortification by lignin synthesis, but it is also an important source of compounds with direct antimicrobial activities.
It is not known, whether it plays a role in PTI against bacteria, but its importance in different plant-fungal interactions has long been shown.
Tobacco plants with suppressed PAL activity - the second enzyme of PPP - have been shown to be more susceptible to the compatible pathogen Cercospora nicotianae .

Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively.
Later on we found that this local resistance can not only be induced by heat-killed pathogens, but also by saprobic bacteria and some hrp/hrc mutants; i.
e. pathogens mutated in their hypersensitivity and pathogenicity genes, many of which code for elements of the Type III Protein Secretion System (T3SS).
Based on our previous findings we regard the local resistance of tobacco as a manifestation of PTI.
Hrp/hrc mutants of the Pseudomonas syringae pv.

syringae 61 bacterial strain (original wild type causing brown spot on bean and HR on tobacco) are damaged in their ability to cause disease on bean or HR on tobacco to varying extent, depending on which hrp gene is turned off.
In this study we sought to better understand the molecular mechanisms underlying the physiological and biochemical processes of tobacco PTI.
Especially the ones leading to inhibition of bacteria, as the exact way by which the plant achieves symptomless arrest of bacterial growth is yet unknown.
At first we identified over 400 EST-s representing genes that are activated during PTI using suppression subtractive hybridization (SSH).
Following assembly of the EST sequences into 176 contigs, their transcriptional activation was confirmed by cDNA microarray and real time RT-PCR.

A highly represented group in our library, that of the phenylpropanoid genes, was strongly implicated to play an important role in mediating the antimicrobial effects of PTI, as shown by transcriptional (microarray and RT-PCR) data, as well as HR-inhibition assays using pharmacological inhibition of a key enzyme of the phenylpropanoid pathway.

Signal transduction-related genes were selected for further transcriptional investigation as this group of genes might mean a key to the regulation of the development and action of PTI.

Real Time RT-PCR also confirmed activation of the same genes by the most examined elicitor of PTI, the flg22 peptide (1 µM) (normalized with actin and a water treated control), as indicated in Figure 3C and Information S3.
Magnitude of this activation was lower and seemed to fade away earlier than in P.

Piperonilic acid (PIP) was chosen, known to be a selective, quasi-irreversible inhibitor of C4H.
Although PIP is toxic to bacteria to a certain extent, it is metabolized fast in planta .
Our in vitro co-incubation experiments showed that proliferation of P.
syringae pv.
syringae 61 incubated in 1 mM PIP solution for 30 minutes was lowered by two orders of magnitude.
In a dilution series of PIP, 50 µM was the first concentration not to influence bacterial proliferation (data not shown).

We tested if these characteristics could affect our planned experiments and found that it had no adverse effects on HR-development if bacteria were injected at least 2 hours after PIP administration (Information S4 A).

Thin layer chromatography (TLC) was applied to measure the levels of cinnamic acid, the second phenolic acid in the phenylpropanoid pathway and precursor of p-coumaric acid, moreover substrate of the key rate limiting enzyme of the pathway: C4H (Figure 6A).
Levels of the product, p-coumarate were also measured in tobacco leaf tissue in response to treatments with PTI inducing bacteria (P.

Tobacco leaves were treated with dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), piperonylic acid (PIP), P.

Tissue samples (500 mg) were collected 6 hours after infiltration.

This level was generally elevated upon any type of treatment with the C4H inhibitor PIP, either alone or combined with the PTI elicitors.
Elevation compared to control (DMSO) treatment was not significant in the case of PIP treatment alone, but it was definitely significant when PIP was combined with either PTI elicitor.

Looking at pairs of treatment X and treatment X+PIP, the largest difference was found between hrcC and hrcC+PIP treatment among our experimental settings, and difference between flg22 and flg22+PIP was also significant.
p-Coumaric acid is directly synthetized from cinnamic acid in plants by C4H.
p-Coumaric acid (Figure 6A) was not detectable in samples inoculated only with DMSO or PIP (negative controls), as opposed to the other samples inoculated with either one of the PTI elicitors P.

We would have liked to see how changes in transcriptional levels relate to changes in metabolite levels in Figure 6A.
We expected that if PIP is supposed to inhibit PTI through inhibiting C4H, than the plant might try to counteract its effect on a transcriptional level.
This would mean that PIP addition would elevate transcriptional levels of some PTI-related PPP genes, or at least of C4H.

In general, we saw again that P.
s.
